ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for ban mian with pork dumplings; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle soup dish' (FOODON_03315671) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as closest defensible category; no exact code exists for Singaporean ban mian with pork dumplings.

main_ingredients: leafy vegetables (typically chye sim or spinach)
anchovies (ikan bilis) or pork bone broth

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pork dumpling ban mian soup is approximately 500g, comprising handmade flat wheat noodles, several pork dumplings, leafy greens, and a savoury broth.

nutrient_source.title: Pork dumpling ban mian (soup) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Pork dumpling ban mian (soup)' (cr_id: F05060100). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork dumpling ban mian soup. GI estimated at 65 based on the closest analogous food: fresh wheat noodles / Chinese-style wheat noodles (GI ~65, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The soup broth and protein content from pork dumplings are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (48.95g from 500g serving) / 100 = 31.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 315 mg/100g; a 500g bowl delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium, representing 68–79% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume the broth sparingly or request a reduced-sodium preparation, as the soup broth concentrates sodium significantly; eating noodles and dumplings with minimal broth can reduce total sodium intake by 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 9.79 g/100g from refined wheat flour noodles with a medium GI (~65) and very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g), providing minimal glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard bowl, request additional leafy vegetables to increase fibre, and increase the proportion of dumplings relative to noodles to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 315 mg/100g (high per-serving load), saturated fat from pork mince dumplings, and low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g) with no meaningful omega-3 or antioxidant contribution.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit bowl frequency to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt broth, and consider substituting pork dumplings with prawn or tofu dumplings to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork mince contains arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; dietary fibre is negligible at 0.3 g/100g with no meaningful anti-inflammatory micronutrient contribution.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork dumplings with prawn or tofu dumplings to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request additional leafy greens such as spinach or chye sim to increase antioxidant and fibre content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Dumpling Ban Mian Soup is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of handmade flat wheat noodles (ban mian, literally 'board noodles') served in a savoury broth alongside pork mince dumplings and leafy green vegetables such as chye sim or spinach. The noodles are made fresh by hand-pulling or cutting wheat dough into irregular flat strips, giving them a chewy, slightly dense texture that distinguishes them from machine-extruded noodles. The broth is typically prepared from pork bones or dried anchovies (ikan bilis), producing a light but umami-rich soup base. Per 100g, the dish provides 71 kcal, 3.5g protein, 2.01g fat, and 9.79g carbohydrate, with sodium at 315 mg and dietary fibre at only 0.3g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on fresh wheat noodle analogues. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to the high per-serving sodium load (~1575 mg per 500g bowl), moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined wheat carbohydrates with low fibre buffering, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat from pork. The dish is not halal. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore, and is considered a comfort food with cultural significance in the local Chinese community.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060100) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food identity is exact and the source match is direct.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g (0.7g) is estimated from the fat composition of pork mince and wheat noodles using USDA FoodData Central analogues as a supporting reference, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on fresh Chinese wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for pork dumpling ban mian soup has been published. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' based on the dish's established cultural origin. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (65) × carbohydrate per 500g serving (48.95g) / 100 = 31.8.
